guest_role: Founding Managing Partner at Pear VC
assertiveness_why: Prepared multi-sentence questions with real follow-ups, but never contests claims; primarily receptive and biographical in nature.
question_style: Open-ended biographical questions that invite storytelling; Harry uses prior statements to build follow-ups ("And you said...") rather than challenging; no pushback or alternative perspectives offered; mostly building on guest's narratives.
tone: Very warm and admiring; Harry opens with effusive praise ("most inspiring guest"); formal but friendly; high fandom; self-deprecating humor ("sadly, I didn't write a check").
depth: Surface-level personal narrative with some strategic elements; focuses on Pejman's personal journey and founder assessment patterns; touches on dilution and fund structure but mostly biographical storytelling rather than technical depth.
harry_quotes: So talk to me about how you arrived in Silicon Valley with $700 and a few words of English.
What do you think made you so brilliant at networking with those elite of tech society?
You've said before that you never invest without meeting the co founder.
learnings: In-person relationship building and personal interaction are foundational to early-stage venture success, especially when the investor lacks formal credentials in tech.
Founder character traits like humility, visionary thinking, healthy paranoia, and team cohesion are as critical to investment outcomes as product quality.
Geographic presence in innovation epicenters and willingness to participate authentically in community-building can compound opportunity access for newcomers without traditional backgrounds.
notable: Pejman's remarkable personal backstory (yogurt shop attic, rug salesman, $20B+ portfolio) dominates the episode, making it more biographical narrative than analytical VC interview. Early Harry (2017) still developing interview structure.
